pathogens.name: Mixed (EBV / Ross River virus / Coxiella burnetii)
Campylobacter jejuni / E. coli O157:H7 (Walkerton outbreak)
Gulf War deployment exposures
pathogens.vector: deployment: pyridostigmine bromide, organophosphate pesticides, low-level nerve agent, oil-fire smoke, depleted uranium, multiple vaccines

instruments.name: Rome criteria for irritable bowel syndrome
Brighton Collaboration criteria for Guillain-Barré syndrome

measures.label: Fatigue
measures.boundary_note: Never merged with sym:post-exertional-malaise, even where the source conflates them.

measures.label: Post-exertional malaise
measures.boundary_note: Never merged with sym:fatigue.

measures.label: Cognitive dysfunction
measures.boundary_note: Concentration/processing/executive impairment; distinct from sym:memory-impairment.

measures.label: Arthralgia / rheumatic musculoskeletal pain
measures.boundary_note: Reported symptom, not radiographic arthritis.

measures.label: Irritable bowel syndrome (post-infectious)
measures.boundary_note: Rome-criteria IBS as a GI symptom-cluster outcome.

measures.label: Chronic fatigue syndrome / ME-CFS caseness
measures.boundary_note: Meeting a formal CFS case definition (Fukuda/CDC etc.), a symptom-cluster outcome distinct from the single symptom sym:fatigue.

measures.label: CFS-like illness
measures.boundary_note: Author-defined CFS-like caseness that does not claim a formal case definition.

measures.label: Felt fully recovered
measures.boundary_note: Self-reported global recovery.

measures.label: Any persistent symptom
measures.boundary_note: A composite 'one or more symptoms' outcome; not a specific symptom and not comparable to single-symptom prevalences.

measures.label: Narcolepsy (incident)
measures.boundary_note: Incident diagnosed narcolepsy, typically an incidence rate.

measures.label: Guillain-Barré syndrome
measures.boundary_note: Acute post-infectious immune neuropathy.

measures.label: Skeletal muscle pathology (biopsy)
measures.boundary_note: Tissue-level muscle findings from biopsy; typically qualitative or paired_change (pre/post exertion), not a prevalence.

measures.label: Exercise intolerance
measures.boundary_note: Reduced tolerance of exertion; related to but not identical with sym:post-exertional-malaise (which is delayed symptom worsening).

measures.label: Interleukin-6 (elevated)
measures.boundary_note: Stored here as the proportion of the cohort with an elevated value; the raw distribution would be a mean_sd observation.

measures.label: Free tri-iodothyronine (low)
measures.boundary_note: Proportion with a low fT3.

measures.label: Serum neurofilament light chain (elevated)
measures.boundary_note: Marker of axonal injury; stored as proportion above a percentile threshold.

measures.label: Orthostatic intolerance
measures.boundary_note: Symptom of intolerance to upright posture; distinct from physio:tilt-table-drop which records the haemodynamic response.

measures.label: Widespread pain
measures.boundary_note: Pain above and below the waist, left and right sides, and axial; the core fibromyalgia symptom. Not to be merged with sym:myalgia (muscle-specific) or sym:arthralgia (joint-specific).

measures.label: Tilt-table haemodynamic response
measures.boundary_note: Haemodynamic parameters during head-up tilt testing: heart rate, blood pressure, cerebral blood flow velocity. Typically mean_sd or paired_change (supine→tilt).

measures.label: 2-day CPET workload drop
measures.boundary_note: Reduction in workload at anaerobic threshold or VO2 peak between day-1 and day-2 CPET; a paired_change observation. The hallmark PEM physiology finding.

measures.label: Bell CFS disability scale
measures.boundary_note: A 10-point (0-100) functional disability scale specific to ME/CFS, from 'bedridden' (10) to 'no symptoms with exercise' (100). Categorised into severe/moderate/mild or used as mean_sd.

measures.label: Canadian Consensus Criteria for ME/CFS (CCC 2003)
measures.boundary_note: The 2003 Canadian Consensus Criteria for ME/CFS (Carruthers et al.); requires PEM, sleep disturbance, pain, and neurological/cognitive manifestations plus autonomic, neuroendocrine, or immune symptoms. More restrictive than Fukuda.

measures.label: International Consensus Criteria for ME (ICC 2011)
measures.boundary_note: The 2011 International Consensus Criteria defining ME (Carruthers et al.); requires post-exertional neuroimmune exhaustion (PENE) as the cardinal feature, plus neurological, immune, energy metabolism, and autonomic manifestations. Fatigue is not required — PENE is the hallmark.

measures.label: Institute of Medicine / NAM ME/CFS criteria (IOM 2015)
measures.boundary_note: The 2015 IOM/NAM diagnostic criteria (now NAM). Core triad: (1) substantial fatigue/impairment ≥6 months, (2) post-exertional malaise, and (3) unrefreshing sleep, plus either cognitive impairment OR orthostatic intolerance. Renamed SEID (systemic exertion intolerance disease), though adoption of the name has been limited.

measures.label: Fukuda/CDC 1994 CFS criteria
measures.boundary_note: The 1994 CDC/Fukuda research case definition: ≥6 months of clinically evaluated, unexplained persistent or relapsing chronic fatigue that is not substantially alleviated by rest, plus ≥4 of 8 ancillary symptoms (PEM, unrefreshing sleep, impaired memory/concentration, muscle pain, multi-joint pain, headache, sore throat, tender lymph nodes). Does not require PEM.

factors.label: Symptom count at presentation
native_scales: number of acute symptoms in first week

factors.label: Time from onset to presentation
native_scales: days from symptom onset to care

cohorts.name: Amsterdam Long COVID muscle-biopsy PEM cohort (Appelman)
aliases: Amsterdam UMC long COVID exercise/biopsy study
cohorts.time_zero_definition: Established long COVID with exertion-triggered post-exertional malaise; assessed before and after an exercise challenge
cohorts.notes: Small longitudinal case-control study (25 long COVID patients with PEM vs 21 healthy controls) and the only cohort in this database with tissue-level objective measures: a two-day cardiopulmonary exercise test to induce post-exertional malaise, with skeletal muscle biopsies before and after. It documented exercise-induced myopathy, metabolic disturbance, and amyloid-containing deposits that worsened after PEM. Included for the objective-measures and PEM-assessment gap matrices rather than for symptom prevalence; not denominator-defined for prevalence estimation.
cohorts.symptom_instrument_note: Selected specifically for post-exertional malaise; not a general symptom-prevalence survey.

publications.title: Muscle abnormalities worsen after post-exertional malaise in long COVID
publications.authors: Appelman B, Charlton BT, Goulding RP, et al.

observations.measure_verbatim: exercise-induced myopathy, metabolic disturbance and amyloid-containing deposits in skeletal muscle, worsening after post-exertional malaise

value.text: Muscle biopsies after a two-day CPET-induced post-exertional malaise showed severe exercise-induced myopathy (atrophic fibres, focal necrosis), local and systemic metabolic disturbance, and amyloid-containing deposits; abnormalities were worse post-PEM than at baseline. No prevalence is reported — this is a mechanistic tissue finding.

provenance.source_locator: Abstract / Results (muscle biopsy pre/post exercise)

cohorts.name: Bergen COVID-19 home-isolated cohort (Blomberg)
cohorts.denominator_method: 82% of all laboratory-confirmed COVID-19 cases in Bergen during the first pandemic wave, enrolled near acute infection; comprises 247 home-isolated and 65 hospitalised patients.
cohorts.time_zero_definition: Acute PCR-confirmed SARS-CoV-2 infection
funders: Helse Vest (Western Norway Regional Health Authority)
Ministry of Health and Care Services, Norway
cohorts.notes: Notable because it captured a near-complete community denominator (82% of local cases) and included predominantly mild, home-isolated patients, showing that persistent symptoms at 6 months affect even young adults with mild acute disease. No unexposed comparator.

publications.title: Long COVID in a prospective cohort of home-isolated patients
publications.authors: Blomberg B, Mohn KG, Brokstad KA, et al.

observations.measure_verbatim: any persistent symptom at 6 months (whole cohort)

Results (persistent symptoms independently associated with increased convalescent antibody titres)

cohorts.name: Bergen Giardiasis Cohort
cohorts.denominator_method: Individuals with laboratory-confirmed Giardia during the 2004 Bergen waterborne outbreak, compared with matched controls from the same population.
cohorts.time_zero_definition: Laboratory-confirmed acute giardiasis during the 2004 outbreak
funders: Haukeland University Hospital / University of Bergen
cohorts.notes: Controlled cohort with a matched unexposed comparator and unusually long follow-up (3, 6, and 10 years), demonstrating persistence of post-infectious IBS and chronic fatigue after a protozoan (non-viral) infection.

publications.title: Irritable bowel syndrome and chronic fatigue 6 years after giardia infection: a controlled prospective cohort study
publications.authors: Hanevik K, Wensaas KA, Rortveit G, et al.

observations.measure_verbatim: irritable bowel syndrome 6 years after giardiasis

provenance.source_locator: Results/Abstract (IBS 39.4% in exposed, significantly elevated vs controls)

observations.measure_verbatim: chronic fatigue 6 years after giardiasis

provenance.source_locator: Results/Abstract (chronic fatigue 30.8% in exposed, significantly elevated vs controls)

cohorts.name: Brisbane Ross River Virus Polyarthritis Cohort (Mylonas)
aliases: Ross River virus natural history cohort
cohorts.denominator_method: Patients diagnosed with Ross River virus disease by general practitioners (clinical symptoms plus paired serology) in greater Brisbane, followed prospectively.
cohorts.time_zero_definition: Diagnosis of acute Ross River virus disease
cohorts.notes: Prospective questionnaire-based natural-history study following joint symptoms and quality of life to 12 months; showed progressive resolution over 3–6 months in most patients. Enrolled sample size not captured here (pending human extraction from the primary paper).

publications.title: Natural history of Ross River virus-induced epidemic polyarthritis
publications.authors: Mylonas AD, Brown AM, Carthew TL, et al.

cohorts.name: Chicago adolescent post-mononucleosis cohort (Katz)
cohorts.denominator_method: Consecutive adolescents (12-18 y) with serologically confirmed acute infectious mononucleosis, enrolled at diagnosis and followed prospectively.
cohorts.time_zero_definition: Serologically confirmed acute infectious mononucleosis
cohorts.notes: Strong prospective inception cohort in adolescents with laboratory-confirmed acute EBV mononucleosis and structured re-screening for CFS caseness at fixed timepoints. Shows a declining post-infective CFS rate over two years (13% -> 7% -> 4%), one of the cleanest demonstrations of resolving post-viral fatigue.

publications.title: Chronic fatigue syndrome after infectious mononucleosis in adolescents
publications.authors: Katz BZ, Shiraishi Y, Mears CJ, Binns HJ, Taylor R.

observations.measure_verbatim: met criteria for chronic fatigue syndrome 6 months after infectious mononucleosis

provenance.source_locator: Results/Abstract (13% at 6 months)

observations.measure_verbatim: met criteria for chronic fatigue syndrome 12 months after infectious mononucleosis

provenance.source_locator: Results/Abstract (7% at 12 months)

observations.measure_verbatim: met criteria for chronic fatigue syndrome 24 months after infectious mononucleosis

provenance.source_locator: Results/Abstract (4% at 24 months)

cohorts.name: Colombo Dengue Study (CDS)
cohorts.denominator_method: Adults presenting with acute febrile illness (<3 days) clinically suspected as dengue; dengue confirmed vs non-dengue by laboratory testing.
cohorts.time_zero_definition: Onset of acute febrile illness
cohorts.notes: Prospective cohort with a same-setting non-dengue febrile comparator, enabling a relative-risk estimate for post-infection fatigue. Short (2-month) follow-up captures early convalescence only.

publications.title: Dengue and post-infection fatigue: findings from a prospective cohort—the Colombo Dengue Study
publications.authors: Sigera PC, Rajapakse S, Weeratunga P, et al.
publications.journal: Transactions of the Royal Society of Tropical Medicine and Hygiene

observations.measure_verbatim: fatigue at 2 months post-infection

provenance.source_locator: Results/Abstract (51/158 dengue patients; RR 4.93, 95% CI 2.3–10.4 vs non-dengue)
Results (post-infection fatigue risk higher in female dengue patients)

cohorts.name: Dubbo Infection Outcomes Study (DIOS)
cohorts.denominator_method: Consecutive patients presenting to primary care with acute EBV, Ross River virus, or Q fever confirmed by serology, enrolled at acute infection.
cohorts.time_zero_definition: Onset of acute, serologically confirmed infection
funders: National Health and Medical Research Council (Australia)
cohorts.notes: Landmark prospective inception cohort showing a stereotyped post-infective fatigue syndrome across three unrelated pathogens, with risk predicted by acute-illness severity rather than the specific microbe. Compared across pathogens; no unexposed control group.

publications.title: Post-infective and chronic fatigue syndromes precipitated by viral and non-viral pathogens: prospective cohort study
publications.authors: Hickie I, Davenport T, Wakefield D, et al.

publications.title: Postinfective fatigue syndrome is not associated with altered cytokine production
publications.authors: Vollmer-Conna U, Cameron B, Hadzi-Pavlovic D, et al.

observations.measure_verbatim: met diagnostic criteria for a chronic fatigue syndrome at 6 months

provenance.source_locator: Results (approx. 11% met CFS criteria at 6 months)

cohorts.name: Finland Pandemrix childhood narcolepsy cohort (Nohynek)
cohorts.denominator_method: All children living in Finland born 1 Jan 1991 - 31 Dec 2005, with vaccination status from the national vaccination registry and narcolepsy cases from national health registries.
cohorts.time_zero_definition: Receipt (or non-receipt) of AS03-adjuvanted Pandemrix H1N1 vaccine during the 2009-10 campaign
funders: National Institute for Health and Welfare (THL), Finland
cohorts.notes: Population registry-linkage cohort covering all Finnish children in the birth range, comparing narcolepsy incidence between Pandemrix-vaccinated and unvaccinated children (vaccine coverage 75%). Included to span the vaccine trigger class: the strongest example of an immune-mediated (autoimmune) post-exposure sequela with a defined denominator and an unvaccinated comparator. Of 67 confirmed cases, 46 vaccinated and 7 unvaccinated entered the primary analysis.

publications.title: AS03 adjuvanted AH1N1 vaccine associated with an abrupt increase in the incidence of childhood narcolepsy in Finland
publications.authors: Nohynek H, Jokinen J, Partinen M, et al.

observations.measure_verbatim: narcolepsy incidence 9.0 per 100 000 person-years in vaccinated vs 0.7 in unvaccinated children (rate ratio 12.7)

provenance.source_locator: Results/Abstract (rate ratio 12.7, 95% CI 6.1-30.8)

cohorts.name: French Polynesia Zika–Guillain-Barré case-control study
cohorts.time_zero_definition: Onset of Guillain-Barré syndrome (cases); preceding transient illness compatible with Zika ~6 days earlier
cohorts.notes: Case-control design (42 GBS cases; two control groups of 98 age/sex/island-matched patients and 70 Zika-positive patients without neurological signs). Establishes the causal link between Zika infection and an acute post-infectious immune sequela (GBS) rather than a chronic fatigue/pain syndrome; included to span the immune-neurological end of the PAIS spectrum. No longitudinal follow-up of chronic outcomes in this report.

publications.title: Guillain-Barré Syndrome outbreak associated with Zika virus infection in French Polynesia: a case-control study
publications.authors: Cao-Lormeau VM, Blake A, Mons S, et al.

observations.measure_verbatim: Guillain-Barré syndrome patients with serological evidence of recent Zika virus infection (anti-ZIKV IgM or IgG)

provenance.source_locator: Results (41/42 GBS cases had anti-ZIKV IgM or IgG)

cohorts.name: Hong Kong SARS survivors 4-year follow-up (Lam)
cohorts.denominator_method: Survivors of laboratory/clinically diagnosed SARS from the 2003 Hong Kong outbreak, assessed at a single 4-year follow-up.
cohorts.time_zero_definition: Acute SARS illness in 2003
cohorts.notes: Cross-sectional 4-year follow-up of the 2003 SARS-CoV-1 survivors, showing high rates of chronic fatigue and a subset meeting CFS criteria years after the acute illness. A single-timepoint survivor survey with no acute-phase or unexposed comparison, but historically important as a coronavirus antecedent to Long COVID.

publications.title: Mental morbidities and chronic fatigue in severe acute respiratory syndrome survivors: long-term follow-up
publications.authors: Lam MH, Wing YK, Yu MW, et al.

observations.measure_verbatim: chronic fatigue at ~4 years after SARS

provenance.source_locator: Results (40.3% chronic fatigue)

observations.measure_verbatim: met modified CDC criteria for chronic fatigue syndrome at ~4 years after SARS

provenance.source_locator: Results (27.1% met CFS criteria)

cohorts.name: Jinyintan (Wuhan) COVID-19 discharge cohort (Huang)
cohorts.denominator_method: Consecutive adults with laboratory-confirmed COVID-19 discharged from Jin Yin-tan Hospital, Wuhan, between 7 Jan and 29 May 2020.
cohorts.time_zero_definition: Symptom onset of acute COVID-19 (assessed at 6 months, 1 year, and 2 years)
funders: National Natural Science Foundation of China
cohorts.notes: Large prospective survivor cohort with matched community controls at later timepoints. Integrity caveat: the 6-month paper received a Lancet Expression of Concern (2022) and was retracted and republished (2023) after some variables were found to have been disordered; the principal conclusions were upheld in the republished version and in the 1- and 2-year follow-ups. Retained here with this replication note precisely because the database records integrity status rather than hiding it.

publications.title: 6-month consequences of COVID-19 in patients discharged from hospital: a cohort study
publications.authors: Huang C, Huang L, Wang Y, et al.

publications.title: Health outcomes in people 2 years after surviving hospitalisation with COVID-19: a longitudinal cohort study
publications.authors: Huang L, Li X, Gu X, et al.

observations.measure_verbatim: fatigue or muscle weakness at 6 months after symptom onset

provenance.source_locator: Results/Abstract (fatigue or muscle weakness 63%, 1038 of 1655)

observations.measure_verbatim: fatigue or muscle weakness

provenance.source_locator: Results (fatigue or muscle weakness 63%, 1038 of 1655)

cohorts.name: Johns Hopkins SLICE early-Lyme cohort (Aucott/Rebman)
aliases: Study of Lyme Immunology and Clinical Events (SLICE)
cohorts.denominator_method: Patients with physician-diagnosed early Lyme disease (erythema migrans), ideally treated, enrolled at diagnosis; a group of endemic-area participants without Lyme disease serves as the comparison.
cohorts.time_zero_definition: Diagnosis and antibiotic treatment of early Lyme disease
cohorts.notes: Prospective inception cohort of ideally-treated early Lyme disease with a non-Lyme comparison group and a bio-repository, designed specifically to estimate the incidence of post-treatment Lyme disease. Functionally-impairing persistent symptoms occurred in 14% of treated patients vs 4% of controls, giving a rigorous denominator for PTLD that most Lyme literature lacks.

publications.title: Risk of post-treatment Lyme disease in patients with ideally-treated early Lyme disease: a prospective cohort study
publications.authors: Aucott JN, Yang T, Yoon I, Powell D, Geller SA, Rebman AW.

observations.measure_verbatim: functionally-impairing persistent symptoms (post-treatment Lyme disease) after ideal treatment of early Lyme disease vs non-Lyme controls

provenance.source_locator: Results (14% of treated Lyme patients vs 4% of controls)

cohorts.name: London post-glandular-fever fatigue cohort (White)
cohorts.denominator_method: Consecutive patients presenting with glandular fever (EBV) or an ordinary upper respiratory tract infection, enrolled at acute presentation and followed prospectively; the URTI group serves as the comparison.
cohorts.time_zero_definition: Acute glandular fever (or comparison upper respiratory tract infection)
cohorts.notes: Landmark prospective inception cohort with an active comparison group (ordinary URTI), showing that an acute post-infective fatigue syndrome is markedly more common after glandular fever than after a routine URTI. A chronic fatigue syndrome persisted in a minority (~9-10%) at 6 months. Establishes specificity of the trigger, not just occurrence.

publications.title: Incidence, risk and prognosis of acute and chronic fatigue syndromes and psychiatric disorders after glandular fever
publications.authors: White PD, Thomas JM, Amess J, Crawford DH, Grover SA, et al.

observations.measure_verbatim: acute fatigue syndrome at onset after glandular fever (47%) vs after ordinary upper respiratory tract infection (20%)

provenance.source_locator: Results/Abstract (acute fatigue syndrome 47% GF vs 20% URTI)

cohorts.name: Netherlands Q-fever outbreak cohort (Morroy)
cohorts.denominator_method: Notified acute Q-fever patients from the 2007-2008 Dutch outbreaks, surveyed by postal questionnaire 12-26 months after illness onset; health status compared with Dutch reference norms.
cohorts.time_zero_definition: Onset of notified acute Q-fever illness
funders: Netherlands National Institute for Public Health and the Environment (RIVM)
cohorts.notes: Large registry-based survey following the world's largest documented Q-fever outbreak, measuring long-term fatigue and quality of life 1-2 years after acute illness with the Nijmegen Clinical Screening Instrument. A denominator-defined bacterial (non-viral) post-infectious fatigue cohort. Cross-sectional postal survey design; compared with population norms rather than a concurrent unexposed control.

publications.title: The health status of Q-fever patients after long-term follow-up
publications.authors: Morroy G, Peters JB, van Nieuwenhof M, et al.

observations.measure_verbatim: severely affected by fatigue 12-26 months after acute Q-fever

provenance.source_locator: Results/Abstract (fatigue severely affected 43.5%)

observations.measure_verbatim: severely affected general quality of life 12-26 months after acute Q-fever

provenance.source_locator: Results/Abstract (general quality of life severely affected 44.9%)

cohorts.name: NIH Intramural ME/CFS Deep-Phenotyping Study (Walitt/Nath 2024)
cohorts.denominator_method: Extensively screened ME/CFS volunteers meeting IOM 2015 criteria plus matched healthy controls, recruited to the NIH Clinical Center for a multi-day inpatient deep-phenotyping protocol.
cohorts.time_zero_definition: Not applicable (cross-sectional deep-phenotyping with retrospective illness duration)
cohorts.contact: Avindra Nath / Brian Walitt, NIH NINDS
funders: National Institutes of Health Intramural Research Program (NINDS)
cohorts.data_availability: Individual-level data available via NIH data-sharing policies
cohorts.notes: The single most intensively measured ME/CFS cohort in existence. 17 ME/CFS participants (IOM 2015 criteria) and 21 matched healthy controls underwent an exhaustive multi-day protocol at the NIH Clinical Center including: 2-day CPET, tilt-table testing, brain MRI/MRS, fMRI, metabolomics, proteomics, immunophenotyping (flow cytometry, CyTOF, cytokine panels), stool microbiome, muscle biopsy with RNA-seq and mitochondrial respirometry, CSF lumbar puncture with proteomics and metabolomics, overnight polysomnography, and extensive neuropsychological testing. Despite the small N (~40), this is the highest-value Tier-1 cohort for physiologic and lab measures. The study found no consistent immunological signature discriminating cases from controls but documented significant effort preference and central motor activation differences. Placed under unknown-trigger as participants had both post-infectious and gradual onset. A contested study in the patient community due to the interpretation of the CPET and effort-preference findings; the database records the observations regardless of interpretive controversy.

publications.title: Deep phenotyping of post-infectious myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome
publications.authors: Walitt B, Singh K, LaMunion SR, et al.

cohorts.symptom_instrument_note: Extensive battery: SF-36, PROMIS fatigue, COMPASS-31, McGill Pain Questionnaire, Multidimensional Fatigue Inventory (MFI-20), neuropsychological test battery, structured clinical history

observations.measure_verbatim: met IOM 2015 (NAM) diagnostic criteria for ME/CFS

provenance.source_locator: Walitt 2024, Table 1 (17 ME/CFS participants met IOM 2015 criteria)

observations.measure_verbatim: SF-36 vitality subscale score

provenance.source_locator: Walitt 2024, Supplementary Tables (SF-36 administered to all participants; individual scores extractable from supplementary data)

observations.measure_verbatim: 2-day CPET workload at ventilatory anaerobic threshold (VAT) — change from day 1 to day 2

provenance.source_locator: Walitt 2024, Results (2-day CPET performed; day-1 to day-2 VAT workload change extractable from supplementary data)

observations.measure_verbatim: tilt-table haemodynamic response (heart rate and blood pressure change from supine to 70° head-up tilt)

provenance.source_locator: Walitt 2024, Methods (tilt-table testing was part of the protocol; haemodynamic parameters extractable from supplementary data)

observations.measure_verbatim: Multi-omics biospecimen inventory: serum, plasma, PBMC, whole blood, CSF, muscle biopsy, stool, saliva, urine from all participants

value.text: Extensive multi-omics sampling from all 57 participants: serum, plasma, PBMC, whole blood (flow cytometry, CyTOF, cytokine panels), CSF via lumbar puncture (proteomics, metabolomics), skeletal muscle biopsy (RNA-seq, mitochondrial respirometry), stool (microbiome), saliva, urine. Banked at NIH Clinical Center biorepository.

provenance.source_locator: Walitt 2024, Methods (full protocol description details all specimen types collected)

provenance.source_locator: Walitt 2024 (IOM 2015 criteria require PEM; all 17 ME/CFS participants met IOM criteria, therefore all have PEM)

cohorts.name: NIH RECOVER-Adult Cohort
cohorts.denominator_method: Nationwide multi-site US cohort enrolling SARS-CoV-2 positive and negative adults from >200 sites. Enrolment began in 2021, enrolling both acute and post-acute participants with retrospective infection ascertainment and prospective follow-up.
cohorts.time_zero_definition: Date of first positive SARS-CoV-2 PCR or antigen test
cohorts.storage: Centralised RECOVER biorepository at multiple academic medical centres
cohorts.data_availability: De-identified data available via NIH data-sharing platform; biospecimens via application to RECOVER biorepository
cohorts.notes: The flagship US Long COVID study: the largest prospective, controlled Long COVID cohort globally. Enrols SARS-CoV-2 positive adults across the full spectrum (asymptomatic through severe acute COVID) plus SARS-CoV-2 negative controls from the same communities. Standardised protocol across >200 sites with harmonised data collection, imaging, and biospecimen banking. Acute-phase and convalescent specimens banked — this is the most important specimen-banking Long COVID cohort. Multiple substudies are publishing as the cohort matures; add publications and observations iteratively as substudies report. The design is prospective but non-inception (many participants enrolled post-acutely with retrospective infection date); this is recorded in the design field. The denominator is defined (all enrolled with confirmed SARS-CoV-2 status) but the source population denominator is the catchment areas of the 200+ sites, not a closed population.

publications.title: Researching COVID to Enhance Recovery (RECOVER) adult study protocol: Rationale, objectives, and design
publications.authors: Horwitz LI, Thaweethai T, Brosnahan KB, et al.

cohorts.symptom_instrument_note: Standardised symptom inventory across all sites; instruments include PROMIS scales, SF-36, and a RECOVER-specific symptom questionnaire covering ~35 symptoms across multiple domains. Full instruments listed in protocol.

observations.measure_verbatim: Cohort N enrolled: ~15,000 adults (SARS-CoV-2 positive and negative)

provenance.source_locator: Horwitz 2023, Abstract & Methods (~15,000 adults to be enrolled across >200 US sites)

observations.measure_verbatim: Biospecimen banking: acute and convalescent serum, plasma, PBMC, whole blood, urine, saliva from all participants

value.text: Serum, plasma, PBMC, whole blood, urine, and saliva collected at enrolment and at scheduled follow-up visits from all ~15,000 participants. Acute-phase specimens available for those enrolled during acute COVID. Specimens stored at centralised RECOVER biorepository across multiple academic medical centres. Available to external researchers via application to the RECOVER biospecimen access committee.

provenance.source_locator: Horwitz 2023, Methods (biospecimen collection protocol; specimens collected at enrolment and follow-up visits)

observations.measure_verbatim: Controlled design: SARS-CoV-2 positive vs. negative participants from same communities

value.text: RECOVER is the only Long COVID cohort with a national US sampling frame, SARS-CoV-2 negative controls drawn from the same communities, standardised multi-site protocol, acute-phase biospecimens, and 4-year planned follow-up. This makes it the Tier-1 reference cohort for Long COVID.

provenance.source_locator: Horwitz 2023, Methods (SARS-CoV-2 positive and negative participants enrolled from same catchment areas)

cohorts.name: PACVS registry cohort (Semmler/Mundorf)
cohorts.time_zero_definition: First SARS-CoV-2 (mRNA) vaccination; chronic symptoms persisting >=5 months
cohorts.notes: Comparative registry cohort of 191 people reporting post-acute COVID-19 vaccination syndrome (PACVS) vs 89 healthy controls, with serum biomarkers measured before and 6 months after vaccination. Distinguishes the vaccine trigger class (with Nohynek). Self-referred, not denominator-defined, so prevalence figures describe the affected registry, not a population; control-group values for individual markers were not extracted here and are marked measured_not_reported.
cohorts.symptom_instrument_note: Symptom clustering plus serum biomarker panel (interleukins, thyroid hormones, IgG subclasses, iron markers, neurofilament light chain, receptor autoantibodies).

publications.title: Clinical and Diagnostic Features of Post-Acute COVID-19 Vaccination Syndrome (PACVS)
publications.authors: Mundorf AK, Semmler A, Heidecke H, et al.

publications.title: Chronic Fatigue and Dysautonomia following COVID-19 Vaccination Is Distinguished from Normal Vaccination Response by Altered Blood Markers
publications.authors: Semmler A, Mundorf AK, Kuechler AS, et al.

observations.measure_verbatim: malaise and chronic fatigue present in >80% of cases

cohorts.name: Patient-Led Long COVID international cohort (Davis 2021)
aliases: Patient-Led Research Collaborative Long COVID survey
cohorts.time_zero_definition: Self-reported onset of suspected or confirmed COVID-19 (illness lasting >28 days, onset prior to June 2020)
cohorts.conflicts: Authors were members of the patient-led group and many were themselves Long COVID patients.
cohorts.notes: Deliberately included as a methodologically weak but historically pivotal cohort: a large (n=3762, 56 countries) self-selected online convenience sample recruited through patient support communities, with no control group, no laboratory confirmation for most respondents, and self-reported outcomes. Its breadth of symptom characterisation (203 symptoms across 10 organ systems) is unmatched, but the design cannot estimate population prevalence. Useful in the gap matrix to contrast against denominator-defined cohorts.

publications.title: Characterizing long COVID in an international cohort: 7 months of symptoms and their impact
publications.authors: Davis HE, Assaf GS, McCorkell L, et al.

cohorts.name: PHOSP-COVID (UK post-hospitalisation COVID-19)
cohorts.denominator_method: Adults discharged from 39 UK hospitals after confirmed or clinically diagnosed COVID-19 (discharged 5 Mar 2020 - 31 Mar 2021), recruited to a multicentre prospective cohort.
cohorts.time_zero_definition: Hospital discharge following acute COVID-19
cohorts.data_availability: Access via the PHOSP-COVID consortium data-sharing process.
cohorts.notes: Large multicentre prospective cohort of hospitalised COVID-19 survivors with an active biobank and deep phenotyping/inflammation profiling. Full recovery was reported by only about a quarter of participants and did not improve meaningfully between 5 months and 1 year. No unexposed comparison group.

publications.title: Physical, cognitive, and mental health impacts of COVID-19 after hospitalisation (PHOSP-COVID): a UK multicentre, prospective cohort study
publications.authors: Evans RA, McAuley H, Harrison EM, et al. (PHOSP-COVID Collaborative Group)

publications.title: Clinical characteristics with inflammation profiling of long COVID and association with 1-year recovery following hospitalisation in the UK: a prospective observational study
publications.authors: PHOSP-COVID Collaborative Group.

observations.measure_verbatim: felt fully recovered at 5 months after discharge

provenance.source_locator: Results (501 of 1965, 25.5% fully recovered at 5 months)

observations.measure_verbatim: felt fully recovered at 1 year after discharge

provenance.source_locator: Results (232 of 804, 28.9% fully recovered at 1 year)

cohorts.name: Post-Vac-Syndrom Deutschland patient survey
cohorts.time_zero_definition: COVID-19 vaccination; self-reported post-vaccination symptoms
cohorts.conflicts: Run by a patient-advocacy initiative; self-selected respondents recruited from private affected-persons groups and a non-public forum.
cohorts.data_availability: Results published on the initiative's website.
cohorts.notes: Grey-literature patient-organisation survey (n=777, Feb-Jun 2022) of people reporting post-vaccination symptoms in Germany. Not peer-reviewed and with no DOI/PMID, so it is admitted only as a flagged grey-literature source (allowed by a URL-only publication record) to represent the patient-advocacy end of the spectrum honestly. Most figures are subgroup percentages (e.g. among affected women); onset was within one week in 57% and within two weeks in a further 21%. Prevalence figures cannot be interpreted as population estimates.
cohorts.symptom_instrument_note: Self-report symptom checklist across multiple body systems; many figures reported for the female subgroup only.

publications.title: Post-Vac-Syndrom Deutschland: Ergebnisse der Betroffenen-Umfrage (patient survey results)
publications.authors: Post-Vac-Syndrom Deutschland (patient initiative)

observations.measure_verbatim: tingling / numbness sensations (most common symptom; reported by women)

provenance.source_locator: Survey results page (tingling/numbness 57% of women)

cohorts.name: PREVAIL III Ebola Survivor Cohort
aliases: Partnership for Research on Ebola Virus in Liberia III
cohorts.denominator_method: Ebola virus disease survivors (antibody-confirmed) and their close contacts enrolled in Liberia and followed longitudinally.
cohorts.time_zero_definition: Enrolment after recovery from acute Ebola virus disease
funders: National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases (NIAID/NIH)
cohorts.notes: Large observational natural-history cohort with a close-contact comparator, banked specimens, and up to 5 years of 6-monthly follow-up. Documented ocular (uveitis), musculoskeletal, neurologic and other sequelae plus viral persistence in immune-privileged sites. n_enrolled shown is the target survivor count; ~1,126 survivors enrolled by 2017 (plus ~2,700 contacts). Quantitative findings pending human extraction from source tables.

publications.title: A Longitudinal Study of Ebola Sequelae in Liberia
publications.authors: PREVAIL III Study Group

observations.measure_verbatim: uveitis (ocular sequela) significantly more prevalent among survivors than close contacts

provenance.source_locator: Results (uveitis among six symptoms significantly more common in survivors vs contacts; exact percentages pending extraction)

cohorts.name: Réunion chikungunya gendarmes comparative cohort (Marimoutou)
cohorts.denominator_method: Occupational cohort of French gendarmes deployed on Réunion during the 2006 outbreak, classified by CHIKV serostatus; uninfected (CHIK-) gendarmes of the same force serve as an occupationally comparable unexposed group.
cohorts.time_zero_definition: Acute chikungunya infection during the 2006 Réunion outbreak
cohorts.notes: Comparative cohort of 85 CHIKV-infected vs 297 uninfected gendarmes assessed 30 months after the outbreak, with occupationally comparable unexposed controls and lower quality of life plus higher rheumatic, headache and fatigue morbidity in the infected. One of the strongest comparative symptom datasets in the chikungunya literature; per-symptom prevalence percentages are reported in the paper's tables (behind a subscription) and have not yet been extracted, so symptom_findings are intentionally empty pending full-text verification.
cohorts.symptom_instrument_note: Rheumatic, general and quality-of-life symptoms compared between CHIK+ and CHIK-; numeric per-symptom prevalences await full-text extraction.

publications.title: Morbidity and impaired quality of life 30 months after chikungunya infection: comparative cohort of infected and uninfected French military policemen in Reunion Island
publications.authors: Marimoutou C, Vivier E, Oliver M, Boutin JP, Simon F.

cohorts.name: Réunion chikungunya viremic inception cohort (Schilte)
cohorts.denominator_method: Patients with RT-PCR-confirmed acute chikungunya viraemia during the 2006 Réunion outbreak, enrolled at the acute phase and followed for 3 years.
cohorts.time_zero_definition: RT-PCR-confirmed acute chikungunya viraemia
cohorts.notes: Inception cohort defined by acute RT-PCR viraemia (not just serology), with banked acute-phase specimens and 3-year follow-up of chronic arthralgia. A rarer, stronger complement to the serology-ascertained TELECHIK cohort on the same outbreak.

publications.title: Chikungunya virus-associated long-term arthralgia: a 36-month prospective longitudinal study
publications.authors: Schilte C, Staikowsky F, Couderc T, et al.

observations.measure_verbatim: experienced chikungunya-associated arthralgia during the 3-year follow-up

provenance.source_locator: Abstract (about 60% experienced arthralgia over 3 years)

cohorts.name: Sejvar West Nile Neuroinvasive Disease Cohort
cohorts.denominator_method: Patients with West Nile virus neuroinvasive disease confirmed by CSF IgM, enrolled during acute illness.
cohorts.time_zero_definition: Acute West Nile neuroinvasive disease presentation
cohorts.notes: Small prospective inception cohort of confirmed neuroinvasive disease with structured serial assessment to 90 days. Short follow-up and no control group; captures acute-to-subacute neurological recovery.

publications.title: West Nile virus neuroinvasive disease: neurological manifestations and prospective longitudinal outcomes
publications.authors: Sejvar JJ, et al.

observations.measure_verbatim: limb weakness at initial clinical presentation

provenance.source_locator: Results (49% weakness, 35% tremor, 16% cranial neuropathy at presentation)

cohorts.name: TELECHIK cohort (Réunion chikungunya)
cohorts.denominator_method: Random population sample drawn from the SEROCHIK population-based serosurvey on Réunion island; participants classified by CHIKV serostatus (IgG).
cohorts.time_zero_definition: Acute chikungunya infection during the 2005-06 Réunion outbreak (serologically ascertained retrospectively)
cohorts.notes: Population-based cohort with a seronegative comparator group (582 CHIK- vs 512 CHIK+), interviewed on average 2 years after infection (range 15-34 months, median 23.7). One of the few post-infectious cohorts with a true unexposed control drawn from the same population, allowing attributable-fraction estimation (~60% of polyarthralgia attributable to CHIKV). Ascertainment was retrospective/serological, so acute-phase data are absent.

publications.title: Perceived morbidity and community burden after a Chikungunya outbreak: the TELECHIK survey, a population-based cohort study
publications.authors: Gérardin P, Fianu A, Malvy D, et al.

publications.title: Rheumatism and chronic fatigue, the two facets of post-chikungunya disease: the TELECHIK cohort study on Reunion island
publications.authors: Duvignaud A, Fianu A, Bertolotti A, et al.

cohorts.name: Toronto Chronic Post-SARS Cohort (Moldofsky)
cohorts.time_zero_definition: Recovery from acute SARS (assessed 13–36 months later)
cohorts.notes: Deliberately included as a methodologically weak but historically important study: small (22 cases, 7 controls), cross-sectional case-control with a convenience control group, but the first to characterize an ME/CFS/fibromyalgia-like chronic syndrome after a coronavirus — an early precedent for Long COVID.

publications.title: Chronic widespread musculoskeletal pain, fatigue, depression and disordered sleep in chronic post-SARS syndrome; a case-controlled study
publications.authors: Moldofsky H, Patcai J

observations.measure_verbatim: persistent fatigue among post-SARS subjects unable to return to former occupation

provenance.source_locator: Results (chronic fatigue, myalgia, weakness, depression, non-restorative sleep vs healthy controls)

cohorts.name: UK ME/CFS Biobank (CureME, LSHTM)
aliases: London School of Hygiene & Tropical Medicine ME/CFS Biobank
cohorts.denominator_method: Participants recruited via UK NHS specialist CFS services and primary care. Cases met Fukuda and/or Canadian Consensus Criteria. Healthy controls and multiple sclerosis disease controls recruited.
cohorts.time_zero_definition: Not applicable (cross-sectional biobank; illness duration recorded but no inception timepoint)
cohorts.storage: −80 °C at University College London / Royal Free Hospital Biobank
cohorts.contact: CureME team, London School of Hygiene & Tropical Medicine
cohorts.data_availability: Application to biobank access committee required
cohorts.notes: The largest deeply phenotyped ME/CFS biobank with matched healthy and multiple sclerosis disease controls. Participants were clinically assessed and met both Fukuda (CDC 1994) and Canadian Consensus (CCC 2003) criteria for ME/CFS. Specimens include serum, PBMC, RNA, and whole blood — valuable for biomarker discovery. Not a prospective inception cohort; cross-sectional with retrospective illness duration data. Illness duration ranges widely (new-onset to decades). Placed under unknown-trigger as the cohort includes participants with both post-infectious and gradual onset; the database records ME/CFS as the outcome, not the trigger.

publications.title: The UK ME/CFS Biobank for biomedical research on Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/Chronic Fatigue Syndrome (ME/CFS) and Multiple Sclerosis
publications.authors: Lacerda EM, Bowman EW, Cliff JM, et al.

cohorts.symptom_instrument_note: Clinical assessment by trained personnel including structured history, physical examination, and validated questionnaires (SF-36, Chalder Fatigue Scale, Bell CFS disability scale)

observations.measure_verbatim: met Fukuda 1994 CFS criteria

provenance.source_locator: Lacerda 2017, Results (452 CFS cases enrolled)

observations.measure_verbatim: Chalder Fatigue Scale score (bimodal) ≥4

provenance.source_locator: Lacerda 2017, Methods (Chalder Fatigue Scale administered; individual-level data available via biobank application)

provenance.source_locator: Lacerda 2017 (CCC criteria require PEM; all ME/CFS cases met CCC, therefore all have PEM unless sub-analysis by onset type specifies otherwise)

observations.measure_verbatim: unrefreshing sleep (CCC criterion)

provenance.source_locator: Lacerda 2017 (CCC criterion; proportion extractable from individual-level data via biobank application)

observations.measure_verbatim: Biobank specimen inventory: serum, PBMC, RNA, whole blood from 632 participants (452 CFS + 55 MS + 55 healthy + 70 other)

value.text: Serum, PBMC, RNA, whole blood banked at −80 °C from all 632 participants. Available to external researchers via application to the UK ME/CFS Biobank access committee.

provenance.source_locator: Lacerda 2017, Methods & Results (specimen types listed; N=632 total enrolled)

cohorts.name: VITAE vaccine-injury survey (Halma & Varon)
aliases: Vaccination Injury Treatment and Access to Essential Care survey
cohorts.time_zero_definition: COVID-19 vaccination; self-reported vaccine injury / post-vaccination syndrome
cohorts.conflicts: Lead author is affiliated with the Open Source Medicine Foundation, which maintains this database, and with the FLCCC; the survey was distributed through vaccine-injury patient-advocacy networks (self-selected sample).
cohorts.notes: Self-selected online survey (n=706) of people self-identifying as COVID-19 vaccine-injured, focused on disease burden and barriers to care rather than clinical symptom prevalence: chronic symptoms >=6 months in 91%, symptoms >2 years in ~80%, and large economic impact (about 70% of those employed dropped an income category; 26% became unemployed due to their condition). Flagged as a non-peer-reviewed preprint, patient-reported, self-selected, uncontrolled, with an author conflict. Included to represent the patient-advocacy end of the vaccine-trigger spectrum, explicitly labelled as such.
cohorts.symptom_instrument_note: Primarily a barriers-to-care / disease-burden instrument, not a symptom-prevalence survey.

publications.title: Assessing Barriers to Care for the Vaccine Injured: The Vaccination Injury Treatment and Access to Essential Care (VITAE) Survey
publications.authors: Halma M, Varon J.

observations.measure_verbatim: chronic symptoms lasting six months or more post-treatment

provenance.source_locator: Results, Table 3 (chronic symptoms >=6 months in 91.35%)

observations.measure_verbatim: became unemployed due to their condition

provenance.source_locator: Results (26.36% became unemployed due to their condition)

cohorts.name: Walkerton Health Study (post-infectious IBS)
cohorts.outbreak_event: Walkerton waterborne gastroenteritis outbreak, May 2000 (Ontario, Canada)
cohorts.denominator_method: Adult Walkerton residents with no prior chronic GI disorder, classified by whether they had acute gastroenteritis during the May 2000 municipal water contamination; residents without acute GE form the unexposed comparison.
cohorts.time_zero_definition: May 2000 waterborne gastroenteritis outbreak (Campylobacter jejuni / E. coli O157:H7)
funders: Ontario Ministry of Health and Long-Term Care
cohorts.notes: One of the strongest post-infectious cohorts in the database: a whole-community denominator with a within-town unexposed comparator, quantifying post-infectious IBS after bacterial gastroenteritis (36.2% after clinically suspected GE vs 10.1% without). Non-fatigue outcome (IBS), broadening the syndrome coverage. Exposure was ascertained by self-reported acute illness rather than culture, the main limitation.

publications.title: Incidence and epidemiology of irritable bowel syndrome after a large waterborne outbreak of bacterial dysentery
publications.authors: Marshall JK, Thabane M, Garg AX, et al. (Walkerton Health Study Investigators)

observations.measure_verbatim: post-infectious irritable bowel syndrome 2 years after outbreak in those with clinically suspected gastroenteritis vs those without acute gastroenteritis

provenance.source_locator: Results/Abstract (36.2% with suspected GE vs 10.1% without)

cohorts.name: Yale LISTEN post-vaccination syndrome cohort (Krumholz)
cohorts.time_zero_definition: COVID-19 vaccination; self-reported chronic symptoms attributed to it
cohorts.notes: Self-selected online registry (Yale LISTEN) of 241 adults reporting post-vaccination syndrome after COVID-19 immunisation, surveyed a median of ~595 days (about 20 months) post-vaccination; no control group. A weak-design but broad symptom characterisation, with a linked immunological/antigen-signature analysis (Bhattacharjee 2025). Included to represent the self-report end of the vaccine-trigger spectrum; not denominator-defined for prevalence.

publications.title: Post-Vaccination Syndrome: A Descriptive Analysis of Reported Symptoms and Patient Experiences After Covid-19 Immunization
publications.authors: Krumholz HM, Wu Y, Sawano M, et al.

publications.title: Immunological and Antigenic Signatures Associated with Chronic Illnesses after COVID-19 Vaccination
publications.authors: Bhattacharjee B, Lu P, Monteiro VS, et al. (incl. Krumholz HM, Iwasaki A)
